era_context: The High Middle Ages; the Mongol civil war between Kublai Khan and Ariq Böke ongoing; the Mamluk Sultanate under Baibars consolidating power after Ain Jalut (1260); the Reconquista advancing in Iberia; the Papacy engaged in conflict with secular rulers over temporal authority; tensions rising in England between crown and barons.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asia (sparse European sources; local records underrepresented)
Pre-Columbian Americas (no written records in European tradition)
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited documentation outside Mongol administrative records)

events.title: Mongol Civil War Between Kublai Khan and Ariq Böke Continues
events.region: Mongol Empire, East Asia, Central Asia
events.description: The Mongol civil war between Kublai Khan and his brother Ariq Böke, which had begun in 1260, continued in 1262. The conflict was a struggle for succession to the Great Khanate following the death of Möngke Khan in 1259. Kublai Khan, based in the east with support from Mongol forces in China, was gradually gaining the upper hand over Ariq Böke, who controlled the Mongol heartland in Central Asia. The war would continue for several more years, with Ariq Böke eventually being defeated in 1264–1265. This conflict represented a major fracture in Mongol unity and contributed to the eventual division of the Mongol Empire into separate khanates.

sources.name: Yuan Shi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty)
Jami' al-tawarikh (Compendium of Chronicles, Rashid al-Din)

events.certainty_note: The Mongol civil war is well-documented in later compilations, but contemporary sources are limited. The year 1262 falls within the conflict period (1260–1264/1265).

events.title: Baibars' Consolidation of Mamluk Power and Campaigns Against Crusaders
events.description: Sultan Baibars al-Bunduqdari, who had ruled since 1260, continued his military campaigns to consolidate Mamluk control over Egypt and the Levant and to reduce Crusader strongholds. By 1262, Baibars had established himself as a strong military leader and was systematically expanding Mamluk territorial control. His campaigns against the Crusader states were part of a broader strategy to eliminate Christian military presence in the eastern Mediterranean and to establish Mamluk hegemony in the region. Baibars was also consolidating his administrative control and establishing the Mamluk Sultanate as a stable political entity.

sources.name: Ibn al-Athir and later Arab historians

events.certainty_note: Baibars' reign and general military activities are well-documented, but specific campaigns in 1262 are less precisely dated in surviving sources. The year falls within his consolidation period (1260–1277).

events.title: Llywelyn ap Gruffydd's Consolidation of Power in Wales
events.description: Llywelyn ap Gruffydd, Prince of Gwynedd, was consolidating his power over much of Wales by 1262. He had defeated his brother Dafydd and was expanding his authority over other Welsh principalities. By 1262, he was emerging as the dominant Welsh prince, though his position remained contested by English interests and rival Welsh lords. His rise to power represented the last major period of Welsh independence before English conquest under Edward I.
key_figures: Henry III of England (overlord, though with limited effective control)

sources.name: Brut y Tywysogion (Chronicle of the Princes)
Historiographic consensus on Welsh medieval history

events.certainty_note: Llywelyn's rise to dominance in Wales by the early 1260s is well-documented in both Welsh and English sources. The year 1262 marks a key point in his consolidation of power.
cross_references: Part of broader Welsh independence narrative

events.title: Alfonso X of Castile's Continued Reconquista and Intellectual Patronage
events.description: Alfonso X of Castile, known as 'El Sabio' (the Wise), continued his military campaigns against Islamic Granada and other Muslim territories in Iberia, while also serving as a major patron of learning and culture. By 1262, he had established himself as a significant intellectual patron, commissioning translations and compilations of knowledge. His reign represented a synthesis of Christian, Muslim, and Jewish intellectual traditions in medieval Iberia, and his cultural patronage had lasting influence on European learning.
key_figures: Scholars and translators at Alfonso's court

sources.name: Works commissioned by Alfonso (Estoria de Espanya, etc.)

events.certainty_note: Alfonso X's cultural patronage and intellectual activities are well-documented in surviving works and chronicles. His reign (1252–1284) is one of the best-documented periods of medieval Iberian history.
cross_references: Related to medieval intellectual synthesis in Iberia

events.title: Tensions Rise Between English Crown and Barons
events.description: Tensions between King Henry III of England and the baronial nobility were rising in 1262, setting the stage for the Second Barons' War that would erupt in 1263–1264. The disputes centered on royal authority, baronial privileges, the influence of foreign advisors (particularly the Lusignan family), and the implementation of the Provisions of Oxford (1258), which had attempted to limit royal power. By 1262, these tensions had become acute, and the conflict would soon escalate into open warfare.
key_figures: Simon de Montfort, Earl of Leicester (baronial leader)
events.certainty_note: The rising tensions between crown and barons in 1262 are well-documented in contemporary chronicles and administrative records. The year marks the prelude to the Second Barons' War.

disconfirming_evidence: The Mongol civil war is sometimes presented as a clear struggle between 'eastern' and 'western' Mongol factions; however, the conflict involved complex political negotiations and alliances that do not fit neatly into geographic categories. Baibars' campaigns are sometimes presented as uniformly successful; however, his military efforts faced resistance and setbacks, and the Crusader states persisted for another two decades. Llywelyn ap Gruffydd's rise is sometimes presented as inevitable; however, his position remained contested, and his authority was challenged by both English interests and rival Welsh lords. Alfonso X's cultural patronage is sometimes romanticized as a uniquely enlightened synthesis of cultures; however, his reign also saw significant religious tensions and conflicts. The tensions between English crown and barons are sometimes presented as a simple conflict between 'progressive' and 'reactionary' forces; however, both sides were defending what they saw as legitimate rights and privileges.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1262 is good for European events (England, Wales, Iberia) due to abundant contemporary chronicles and administrative records. Mongol sources are primarily later compilations (Yuan Shi, Jami' al-tawarikh) and reflect editorial perspectives of later dynasties. Mamluk sources are available but often less precise about specific dates within a year. The historical record is heavily weighted toward political, military, and religious events; economic and social developments are underrepresented. Non-European regions (Africa, Southeast Asia, the Americas) are essentially absent from surviving written sources for this year. The period leading up to the Second Barons' War is exceptionally well-documented due to the abundance of English administrative records and chronicles.

graph_edges.note: Mongol civil war continued and reached critical point with Ariq Böke's defeat in 1264
Baibars' consolidation of power in 1262 enabled continued campaigns against Crusaders
Llywelyn's consolidation of power in 1262 continued through 1266
Rising tensions between crown and barons in 1262 escalated into Second Barons' War in 1263
